summary: This notebook provides a complete inference pipeline for the BirdCLEF 2025 competition, focusing on generating predictions from pre-trained audio classification models. It demonstrates converting raw audio files into normalized mel-spectrograms, processing them in 5-second sliding windows, and passing them through an EfficientNetV2-S backbone. The pipeline includes loading multiple model checkpoints, averaging their outputs, and applying a temporal smoothing filter to the final submission CSV. Readers should understand how to structure a production-ready inference script for audio tasks, handle variable-length inputs, and refine predictions through post-processing.
competition_flow: Loads test audio files, converts them to mel-spectrograms, runs inference using an ensemble of pre-trained EfficientNetV2-S models, applies temporal smoothing to the predictions, and exports a formatted submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads taxonomy CSV for class labels and sample submission CSV
Iterates over .ogg files in the test directory
Uses librosa.load to extract raw audio waveforms at 32kHz
data_processing: Converts raw audio to mel-spectrograms using librosa with specific FFT/hop/mel parameters
Handles NaN values and pads short audio segments
Normalizes spectrograms to [0,1] and resizes to 256x256 via OpenCV
loss_functions: FocalLossBCE (custom combination of BCEWithLogitsLoss and sigmoid_focal_loss)
ensembling: Averages predictions across multiple loaded model checkpoints; TTA is defined but disabled. Applies a 3-point temporal smoothing filter (weights 0.2, 0.6, 0.2) to predictions within each soundscape before saving the submission.
insights: Mel-spectrograms normalized per-sample and resized to a fixed shape work effectively as input for CNN-based audio classification.
Temporal smoothing of per-window predictions helps reduce prediction jitter and improves submission quality.
A custom FocalLossBCE combining BCE and focal loss components is effective for handling class imbalance in audio classification tasks.
